questions.stem: Why is coordination needed in living systems?

options.text: So component parts collectively perform an overall function

questions.explanation: System integration coordinates component parts so they collectively perform the organism's overall functions.

questions.stem: Which shows the correct hierarchy of subsystems?
questions.explanation: The hierarchy runs cells, tissues, organs and body systems, integrated in a multicellular organism.

questions.scenario: A cheetah is an effective predator because its body systems work together.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Integration of body systems produces emergent properties, such as the cheetah being an effective predator.

questions.stem: Which two systems send messages to integrate organs in animals?

options.text: The nervous system and the endocrine system
The digestive and skeletal systems only

questions.explanation: Organs are integrated by nervous and endocrine (hormonal) signalling, and by transport of materials in the blood.

questions.stem: Which system transports materials between organs?
questions.explanation: The blood system transports materials and energy between organs, integrating the body.

questions.stem: The brain acts as a central organ for:

options.text: Integrating information and for learning and memory

questions.explanation: The brain processes information combined from several inputs and is involved in learning and memory.

questions.stem: The spinal cord is an integrating centre for:
questions.explanation: The spinal cord acts as an integrating centre for unconscious processes, such as reflexes.

questions.stem: Sensory neurons convey messages:

options.text: From receptor cells to the central nervous system

questions.explanation: Sensory neurons carry messages from receptor cells to the central nervous system.

questions.stem: Motor neurons carry messages from the cerebral hemispheres to:
questions.explanation: Motor neurons carry output from the cerebral hemispheres to muscles, stimulating contraction.

questions.stem: A nerve is best described as a:

options.text: Bundle of nerve fibres of sensory and motor neurons

questions.explanation: A nerve is a bundle of nerve fibres from both sensory and motor neurons, with a protective sheath.

questions.scenario: You quickly pull your hand away from a hot object before feeling the pain.
questions.stem: This is an example of a:
questions.explanation: The pain reflex arc is an involuntary response with skeletal muscle as the effector, allowing rapid withdrawal.

questions.scenario: In a pain reflex arc, the signal passes through the spinal cord via:
questions.stem: Which neuron connects the sensory and motor neurons?

options.text: An interneuron in the grey matter

questions.explanation: A single interneuron in the grey matter of the spinal cord links the sensory and motor neurons in the reflex arc.

questions.stem: The cerebellum plays a role in:

options.text: Coordinating skeletal muscle contraction and balance

questions.explanation: The cerebellum coordinates skeletal muscle contraction and balance in the overall control of movement.

questions.scenario: Melatonin is secreted in a daily rhythm.
questions.stem: Which gland secretes melatonin?
questions.explanation: Melatonin is secreted by the pineal gland, modulating sleep patterns as part of circadian rhythms.

questions.stem: Melatonin secretion helps establish a cycle of:
questions.explanation: The diurnal pattern of melatonin helps establish a cycle of sleeping and waking (circadian rhythm).

questions.scenario: The adrenal glands secrete epinephrine (adrenaline).
questions.stem: This prepares the body for:
questions.explanation: Epinephrine from the adrenal glands prepares the body for vigorous activity, facilitating intense muscle contraction.

questions.stem: The endocrine system is controlled largely by the:
questions.explanation: The hypothalamus and pituitary gland control the endocrine system.

questions.scenario: Baroreceptors detect a change in blood pressure.
questions.stem: Baroreceptors monitor:
questions.explanation: Baroreceptors monitor blood pressure; chemoreceptors monitor blood pH and oxygen/carbon dioxide concentrations.

questions.stem: Which brain region coordinates responses that change heart rate?
questions.explanation: The medulla coordinates responses to baroreceptor and chemoreceptor input, sending impulses to change heart rate and stroke volume.

questions.scenario: Blood pH changes are detected by chemoreceptors.
questions.stem: This leads to control of:
questions.explanation: Chemoreceptors in the brainstem detect pH changes and control ventilation rate via the diaphragm and intercostal muscles.

questions.scenario: Food is moved along the gut by peristalsis.
questions.stem: Peristalsis between swallowing and egestion is controlled by the:
questions.explanation: Peristalsis between swallowing and egestion is under involuntary control by the enteric nervous system.

questions.stem: Which digestive actions are under voluntary control by the CNS?
questions.explanation: Initiation of swallowing and egestion is under voluntary CNS control; peristalsis between them is involuntary (ENS).

questions.stem: The difference between conscious and unconscious processes is that unconscious processes:

options.text: Occur without deliberate awareness or control

questions.explanation: Unconscious processes, integrated by the spinal cord, occur without deliberate awareness, unlike conscious processes.

questions.stem: How do the roles of the nervous and endocrine systems in sending messages differ?

options.text: Nervous signals are fast and electrical; endocrine signals are hormonal and slower
Both are identical in speed and method

questions.explanation: The nervous system sends fast electrical messages; the endocrine system uses slower, blood-borne hormonal messages.

questions.scenario: A transverse section of a nerve is examined.
questions.stem: It would show a protective sheath and:
questions.explanation: A nerve section shows a protective sheath enclosing myelinated and unmyelinated nerve fibres.

questions.stem: The brain integrates information by:

options.text: Combining several inputs to process them

questions.explanation: The brain integrates information by processing inputs combined from several sources, and is involved in learning and memory.

questions.scenario: Chemoreceptors detect a fall in blood oxygen.
questions.stem: Chemoreceptors monitor blood pH and:

options.text: Concentrations of oxygen and carbon dioxide

questions.explanation: Chemoreceptors monitor blood pH and oxygen and carbon dioxide concentrations.

questions.stem: Epinephrine has widespread effects that facilitate:
questions.explanation: Epinephrine's widespread effects prepare the body for vigorous activity, facilitating intense muscle contraction.

questions.stem: In the pain reflex arc example, the effector is:
questions.explanation: In the pain reflex arc, skeletal muscle is the effector that produces the rapid withdrawal response.

questions.stem: System integration in living organisms is described as:
questions.explanation: System integration is a necessary process, coordinating parts so the organism functions as a whole.

questions.scenario: A receptor in the skin detects a stimulus.
questions.stem: The message is carried to the CNS by a:
questions.explanation: Sensory neurons carry information from receptor cells to the central nervous system.

questions.stem: Control of ventilation rate involves signals sent to the:
questions.explanation: Chemoreceptor input leads to signals to the diaphragm and intercostal muscles to control ventilation rate.

questions.scenario: A gymnast maintains balance on a beam.
questions.stem: Which brain region is especially involved?
questions.explanation: The cerebellum coordinates skeletal muscle contraction and balance.

questions.stem: Emergent properties of an organism arise from:
questions.explanation: Emergent properties, such as effective predation, arise from the integration of cells, tissues, organs and systems.

questions.stem: The pituitary gland works closely with which brain region to control the endocrine system?
questions.explanation: The hypothalamus and pituitary gland together control the endocrine system.

questions.scenario: A student records how seedlings bend in response to a stimulus.
questions.stem: Recording the angle of curvature is an example of:
questions.explanation: Measuring the angle of curvature gives quantitative data; drawings of the response give qualitative data.

questions.scenario: A plant shoot grows towards a light source coming from one side.
questions.stem: This directional growth response is called:
questions.explanation: Growth of a shoot towards lateral light is positive phototropism.

questions.stem: Phytohormones are signalling chemicals that control:

options.text: Growth, development and responses to stimuli in plants

questions.explanation: Phytohormones are plant signalling chemicals controlling growth, development and responses to stimuli.

questions.scenario: Auxin can diffuse into plant cells but not out of them.
questions.stem: How is auxin then moved directionally through tissue?

options.text: By auxin efflux carriers positioned on one side of cells
By free diffusion in all directions

questions.explanation: Auxin efflux carriers on one side of cells actively transport auxin from cell to cell, concentrating it in part of the plant.

questions.scenario: All cells in a tissue concentrate their auxin efflux carriers on the same side.
questions.stem: The result is that auxin:

options.text: Is transported in one direction and becomes concentrated in part of the plant

questions.explanation: Coordinated positioning of efflux carriers moves auxin directionally, concentrating it in a specific region.

questions.scenario: Auxin promotes secretion of hydrogen ions into the apoplast.
questions.stem: How does this promote cell growth?

options.text: Acidifying the wall loosens cross-links, allowing elongation

questions.explanation: Auxin-driven acidification of the cell wall loosens cross-links between cellulose molecules, facilitating cell elongation.

questions.stem: Concentration gradients of auxin cause phototropism by producing:

options.text: Differences in growth rate on the two sides of a shoot

questions.explanation: Auxin gradients cause the differences in growth rate needed for phototropism, bending the shoot toward light.

questions.scenario: Root tips produce cytokinin and shoot tips produce auxin.
questions.stem: These phytohormones are transported so that they:

options.text: Cytokinin moves to shoots and auxin moves to roots, integrating growth
Both stay where they are made

questions.explanation: Cytokinin from root tips moves to shoots and auxin from shoot tips moves to roots, integrating root and shoot growth.

questions.scenario: Ethylene stimulates fruit ripening, and ripening stimulates more ethylene production.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Ethylene and ripening reinforce each other, an example of positive feedback that makes ripening rapid and synchronized.

questions.stem: The benefit of positive feedback in fruit ripening is that ripening becomes:
questions.explanation: Positive feedback between ethylene and ripening ensures fruit ripening is rapid and synchronized.

questions.scenario: A student draws seedlings to record tropic responses.
questions.stem: These drawings are:
questions.explanation: Diagrams recording observations are qualitative data; measured angles of curvature would be quantitative.

questions.stem: Which statement about phytohormones is correct?

options.text: A variety of chemicals act as phytohormones in plants
Only one chemical acts as a phytohormone
Phytohormones are found only in animals

questions.explanation: A variety of chemicals serve as phytohormones, controlling growth, development and responses to stimuli.

questions.stem: Auxin efflux carriers are an example of maintaining:
questions.explanation: Auxin efflux carriers maintain concentration gradients of the phytohormone auxin across plant tissue.

questions.stem: Interactions between auxin and cytokinin help to:

options.text: Regulate and integrate root and shoot growth

questions.explanation: Auxin and cytokinin interact to ensure root and shoot growth are integrated.

questions.scenario: A shoot bends towards light because cells on the shaded side elongate more.
questions.stem: This difference in growth is driven by:

options.text: A higher auxin concentration on the shaded side

questions.explanation: Auxin accumulates on the shaded side, promoting greater elongation there so the shoot bends toward the light.
